front: What is a Transaction in DBMS?
back: A transaction is a logical unit of work consisting of a sequence of database operations that must be executed as a single atomic unit. It transforms the database from one consistent state to another.

front: Define ACID properties of a transaction
back: ACID stands for Atomicity (all-or-nothing execution), Consistency (valid state transitions), Isolation (concurrent transactions appear serialized), and Durability (committed changes are permanent).

Atomicity ensures that a transaction is treated as a single indivisible unit. Either all operations within the transaction are executed successfully, or none are applied to the database (all-or-nothing property).
Consistency guarantees that a transaction transforms the database from one valid state to another. All integrity constraints must be satisfied after the transaction completes.
Isolation ensures that concurrent transactions execute as if they were serialized (one after another). The intermediate results of a transaction are invisible to other concurrent transactions.
Durability guarantees that once a transaction is committed, its changes persist permanently in the database even if system failure occurs. Updates are stored in non-volatile memory.
1) Active (execution phase) 2) Partially Committed (last statement executed) 3) Committed (successfully completed) 4) Failed (normal execution cannot continue) 5) Aborted (rolled back after failure)
A serial schedule is where transactions are executed one after another without any interleaving of operations. No two transactions execute concurrently. It always maintains database consistency.

Conflict Serializability is a schedule equivalence property where non-serial schedules can be transformed into equivalent serial schedules by swapping non-conflicting operations. Conflicts occur when two transactions access the same data item and at least one writes.

front: What is the Lost Update problem?
back: Lost Update occurs when two concurrent transactions read and modify the same data, and one transaction's updates are overwritten by the other, causing the first update to be lost.
A Dirty Read occurs when a transaction reads data that has been modified by an uncommitted concurrent transaction. If that transaction aborts, the read data is invalid/inconsistent.
Non-Repeatable Read occurs when a transaction reads the same data twice but gets different values because another committed transaction modified the data between the two reads.
Phantom Read occurs when a transaction executes a query twice and gets different sets of rows (phantoms) because another transaction inserted or deleted rows that match the query criteria.

front: What is a Lock in concurrency control?
back: A lock is a mechanism that restricts access to a data item by concurrent transactions. Shared locks (S-lock) allow reading; Exclusive locks (X-lock) allow both reading and writing.

Two-Phase Locking is a concurrency control protocol where transactions must acquire all locks before releasing any lock. It has a Growing Phase (locks acquired) and a Shrinking Phase (locks released).
